topic: A person finds out they were adopted through a DNA test
raw_output:
[story 1]

The crisp white paper felt like sandpaper under my fingertips. I stared at the results, the highlighted sections screaming a truth I hadn't been prepared for. My breath hitched, a shallow, painful catch in my chest. Every muscle in my jaw tightened until it ached. I slowly lowered the sheet, the sound of the paper rustling in the quiet room echoing loudly in my ears. Looking up at the framed photo on the mantelpiece—a smiling family I’d always believed was mine—the image seemed to warp, the colors leaching away into a dull, unwelcome gray. I didn't move to pick up the test kit; instead, I walked to the window, pressing my forehead against the cool glass, trying to physically press the shock out of my system. The silence in the house was suddenly suffocating, thick with unspoken revelations.

[story 2]

"I don't understand," I managed, the words sounding thin and reedy even to my own ears. The technician’s calm, measured tone offered no comfort, only an impenetrable wall of clinical fact. When the final report was presented, the air in the consultation room seemed to thin out. My hands, resting on the edge of the table, began to tremble uncontrollably, a fine tremor running up my forearms. I kept my gaze fixed on the polished wood grain of the desk, refusing to meet anyone's eyes. A hot flush spread across my neck and cheeks, a heat that felt entirely too public. I wanted to bolt, to disappear into the nearest shadow, but my feet felt rooted to the floor. Every nerve ending was screaming, demanding an escape from this stark, undeniable reality laid bare before me.

[story 3]

I crumpled the printout into a tight ball, the sound a sharp, brittle snap. It felt like an insult, a deliberate erasure of everything I thought I knew about my own history. I stood in the middle of the living room, the afternoon light streaming in, illuminating the dust motes dancing in the air—tiny, mocking specks of indifferent existence. My knuckles were white where I gripped the paper, and I could feel the rapid, shallow rhythm of my breathing escalating into something frantic. I wanted to shout, to demand an explanation that made sense, but my throat constricted, refusing to form coherent sound. I looked around the familiar space, at the comfortable furniture, and felt utterly alien, a stranger occupying a life that was suddenly mapped out by genetic code I hadn't chosen.
